title: After six weeks all my tumors have shrunk substantially in my whole body (in a lung 50% or more). Sharing my regime: Keytruda (pembrolizumab), Keto Diet, Prof. Thomas Seyfried.
body:
**Background**

I have been diagnosed with stage 4 lung cancer. Cancer has already metastasized to my pelvic bones and to my lymph nodes. After 2 sessions with Keytruda (IV with Keytruda, 3 weeks interval, IV with keytruda, 3 weeks interval, CT-scan), in just 6 weeks I think I have remarkable results. All the tumors in my body have shrunk, in a lung even 50% of more. 

I am male, 52 years old. Earlier this year, a biopsy was performed and it turned out cancer in my lymph node has 90% PD-L1. High PD-L1 is a sign that immunotherapy with Keytruda will often work to shrink tumors. That is the reason my oncologist chose for immunotherapy with Keytruda. I do not experience any meaningful side effects from Keytruda thus far (is that because of my Keto diet?). 

After the diagnosis, I started a Keto diet after extensive reading. I did this because of Prof. Thomas Seyfried findings after decades of research. Professor Thomas Seyfried concluded that cancer cells are fueled by the fermentation of glucose and glutamine. Professor Thomas Seyfried is a professor at Boston College - biology, genetics and biochemistry. He has 150 peer-reviewed publications.

Prof. Thomas Seyfried propagates, that to fight cancer one must do a low carbohydrate diet in combination with lowering glutamine with a drug. During the phase when cancer cells are at their weakest, you hit the cancer cells with therapies like radiation, chemo etc. Because the cancer cells are at their weakest, you need less therapy in such a phase. Even nontoxic therapies (hyperbaric therapy, high vitamin-C IV dose) are effective according to him. See for example case of Dennis Casey (cancer-free using Thomas Seyfried press-pulse-protocol and hyperbaric therapy, Hippcrates Research Foundation). 

​

**Carbohydrate intake** 

By lowering carbohydrate intake, you will lower the glucose to cancer cells. Cancer cells need a lot of glucose for their energy as they use fermentation. Healthy cells produce 32-36 ATP per glucose molecule, cancer cells produce only 2 ATP (so cancer cells need lots of glucose). 

​

**This is my diet around IV (intravenous) day:** 

5 days before IV, 1000 Kcal, less than 20 grams of carbohydrate 

4 days before IV, 800 Kcal, less than 20 grams of carbohydrate 

3 days before IV, 600 kcal, less than 20 grams of carbohydrate 

2 days before IV, water fasting 

1 day before IV, water fasting 

On IV day, 600 kcal, less than 20 grams of carbohydrate 

1 day after IV, 800 kcal or more, less than 20 grams carbohydrate. 

Note: studies have shown that fasting (water fasting and intermittent fasting) is also good for your immune system (I have immunotherapy, so I need a good immune system). 

​

**2 days after IV and thereafter**

 It’s important that after a while (2 or more days after IV **in my case**), you increase the kcal intake. During cancer therapy your weight is important. Unless you are obese, you can not lose a lot of weight during cancer therapy. Weight is important, especially if you have chemo or radiation therapy. 

2 or more days after IV my diet is 3000 kcal or more, less than 20 grams of carbohydrate – until the cycle before IV starts again. To get the kcal, apart from meat and seafood, I eat lots of butter as this contains low carbohydrate (often 0) and have lots of kcal. Look for tips and sources on the internet for other kinds of food with high kcal and low carbohydrate. 

​

**GKI** 

I measure my glucose ketone index (GKI) at least twice a day with the device as shown in the picture (Keto Mojo, GKI). There is an updated version, called GK+. Make sure you have the compatible strips. 

Prof. Thomas Seyfried recommends having a GKI between 1 and 2 during a therapy phase. I try to get the GKI between 1 and 2 on the days around my IV. My regime is based on information that I have gathered, and that I can live with. Others (incl. Professor Thomas Seyfried) may have a different regime. 

​

**Exercises** 

As per recommendation by Prof. Thomas Seyfried, I also do exercises. I walk a lot (approximately every 2 days 8 km, every other day around 3 km, and I do resistance training for approx 20 minutes each day). I do not however do lots of explosive exercises as I fear these may increase glutamine in my body. 

​

**Glutamine** 

Glutamine can only be lowered by a drug. DON is a known anti-glutamine drug, but it has lots of side effects. DON can only be given under strict control. There are new drugs that target the glutamine metabolism of cancer cells, like DRP-104 (phase I/II of clinical trials).

I had planned to request for DRP-104 if my tumors didn’t shrink, but my tumors shrank substantially. So I didn’t make the request. Also, there are cases of people managing their cancer only by lowering their glucose intake - even becoming cancer-free. Further, requesting an anti-glutamine drug will most likely create resistance with your doctor. 

​

**Final words**

My war is far from over, but first battle has a good outcome. It may be that in the future I don’t have such good outcome. But so far I think my regime is beneficial for me.

Also please understand I am not a physician nor a dietist. So this is not medical advice. 

Do your own research on keto diet, Thomas Seyrfried, GKI etc. There are many resources on the internet, also testimonies of people beating cancer with keto diet in combination with cancer therapy. There are even testimonies of people beating cancer with only keto diet and fasting, without any cancer therapy. 

Always listen to your body, diet regimes could be exhausting. Further, diet regimes could have negative effects. I am sharing my regime and testimony as it could help people fighting cancer. Get a professional dietist or doctor involved in your diet if you can. 

If you mention diets as a cancer fighting tool, most likely your oncologist will be very skeptical. In my case (as with other people doing a keto diet therapy for fighting cancer, and having great results) my oncologist couldn’t formally recommend it, though my oncologist said that I should continue what I was doing given the good results. 

​

Best of luck to all of you, God speed.

​

​

​

​

 

​

 

 

. 

​
